summary: My great-great-grandfather James Lowe was born in Cortachy, Scotland in 1820. This small place was where his story began before he emigrated with his family to Canada in 1836. Cortachy represents his roots and the starting point of his long journey to America.

Scotland was the homeland of my great-great-grandfather James Lowe and his wife Wilhelmina Schlaberg. They both originated from there before moving to Canada in the 1830s. Scotland shaped their early lives and connected them to their Scottish heritage even after they left.

James Lowe and his family settled in Canada near LaChute after emigrating from Scotland in 1836. They lived there for about 20 years, raising eleven children and building a life. Though James was not content with life in Canada, it was where he started his family and met Wilhelmina, his wife.

LaChute, Canada was the place where my great-great-grandfather James Lowe and his family settled after leaving Scotland. They lived on land next to Wilhelmina Schlaberg's family, who later became his wife in 1845. LaChute was home to them for two decades before James set off for America.

In 1867, James Lowe traveled to California with his son James Jr. to find land and establish a new life for the family. After a difficult sea journey and some hard work, including threshing and sawmill labor near Fort Ross, James struggled with illness and the tough conditions. Though he eventually returned to Canada, California was the place where he first tried to make a home in America.

James Lowe and his son James Jr. traveled by oxen cart and train to New York City in May 1867 to begin their sea voyage to California. From there, they boarded the steamship Henry Chauncy to start their long journey west. New York City was the gateway for their pioneering trip across the continent.

The Henry Chauncy was the steamship that James Lowe and his son boarded in New York City in 1867 to sail south along the Atlantic coast. This ship was the first leg of their 22-day journey to California, taking them toward Panama where they would cross the isthmus. The Henry Chauncy carried them into the unknown as pioneers for their family.

James Lowe and his son sailed southward along the Atlantic Ocean after leaving New York City in 1867. This vast ocean was the first major stretch of their journey to California, before crossing Panama and heading up the Pacific coast. The Atlantic was a challenging part of their long voyage to a new life.

During their 1867 journey to California, James Lowe and his son crossed the Isthmus of Panama by railroad after sailing down the Atlantic coast. This crossing was a crucial link between the two oceans before they boarded the steamship Constitution to continue north. Panama was a key transit point in their pioneering trip.

After crossing Panama, James Lowe and his son boarded the steamship Constitution to sail up the Pacific coast to San Francisco. This leg of the journey completed their 22-day voyage from New York City to California in 1867. The Constitution was vital in bringing them to the West Coast to start their new life.

San Francisco was the port where James Lowe and his son arrived after their long 22-day journey in 1867. James Jr. returned there to recover from illness while James Sr. worked near Fort Ross. San Francisco marked the beginning and a refuge point in their challenging attempt to settle in California.

Near Fort Ross, California, James Lowe worked at a sawmill after arriving in 1867. The work was difficult and James was already 47 years old, which made the labor challenging. Fort Ross was where he stayed while his son convalesced in San Francisco during that first winter in America.

On February 15, 1868, James Lowe and his son set sail on the steamship Dakota to return to Canada after their difficult winter in California. This ship carried them back across the continent, marking the end of their first attempt to settle in America. The Dakota was part of their journey home to regroup and try again.

After returning to Canada in 1868, James Lowe moved his entire family to Iowa, where his wife's brother had homesteaded. This move marked the start of their second, more successful attempt to settle in America. Iowa became a new beginning for the family before they eventually moved on to Minnesota.

Minnesota was where James Lowe finally homesteaded and established a permanent home for his family after moving from Iowa. His children grew up there, married, and started families of their own. Minnesota represents the successful outcome of James's long journey and efforts in America.

Avoca, Minnesota is where James Lowe Jr. married Anna Sheerin in 1880. This town was part of the family's life as they settled and grew roots in Minnesota. Avoca connects to the next generation of the Lowe family, including my grandfather George Lowe.

James Lowe, my great-great-grandfather, was born in Cortachy, Scotland in 1820. He emigrated to Canada in 1836, married Wilhelmina Schlaberg, and raised eleven children. In 1867, he made a difficult journey to California with his son to find a new life but eventually returned to Canada before successfully settling in Minnesota.

James Lowe Jr., son of James Lowe Sr., accompanied his father on the 1867 journey from Canada to California. He became ill during their time in California and returned to San Francisco to recover. Later, he married Anna Sheerin in 1880 in Avoca, Minnesota, continuing the family line.

Wilhelmina Schlaberg was a fellow Scot and neighbor to James Lowe in LaChute, Canada. They married in 1845 and raised eleven children together. She was the beloved wife of James and the matriarch of the family during their early years in Canada and beyond.

David Lowe was James Lowe Sr.'s father. James returned to Canada in time to see David before he passed away in 1868. This reunion was a poignant moment after James's difficult first attempt to settle in America.
Anna Sheerin married James Lowe Jr. in 1880 in Avoca, Minnesota. She became part of the Lowe family as they established themselves in Minnesota. Anna was the mother of George Lowe, my grandfather.

George Lowe was the son of James Lowe Jr. and Anna Sheerin, and my grandfather. He represents the continuation of the Lowe family line that was established in Minnesota after the long journeys and struggles of his ancestors. Without the family's eventual settlement in Minnesota, I wouldn't be here today.
cues: son of James Jr. and Anna
